title: feat: add health endpoints
body:
# Description

## Changes
- Exposes `/livez` and `/readyz` endpoints when agents are hosted as a service.
- Refactors runtime config updates and agent config resolution so that they use the same coerce/validate/transform/apply logic.
- Creates a agent execution `ToolChoice` enum for consistency.
- Move config resolution logic from the agent to the config classes.
- Extracts agent execution defaults to named constants.

## Issue reference

We strive to have all PR being opened based on an issue, where the problem or feature have been discussed prior to implementation.

Please reference the issue this PR closes: #375

## Checklist

Please make sure you've completed the relevant tasks for this PR, out of the following list:

* [x] Created/updated tests
* [ ] Tested this change against all the quickstarts
* [ ] Extended the documentation
    * [ ] Created the [dapr/docs](https://github.com/dapr/docs) PR: <insert PR link here>

**Note:** We expect contributors to open a corresponding documentation PR in the [dapr/docs](https://github.com/dapr/docs/) repository. As the implementer, you are the best person to document your work! Implementation PRs will not be merged until the documentation PR is opened and ready for review.

TODO:
- [ ] Update docs
- [ ] Update `03-message-router-workflow` example

comments.body: From my understanding, transient errors and non-recoverable errors should all result in a subscription.close() call (through dapr.clients.grpc.subscription.reconnect_stream() and _run_consumer_loop() respectively) which sets the subscription's internal _stream_active flag to False. If this is the right approach, should subscription._is_stream_active() be made public?

Needed a way to surface the consumer statuses to the runner, an alternative could be to keep a "registry" and pass that as an arg, but this is probably better since subscriptions are hidden from the runner?

Since both [app health checks](https://docs.dapr.io/operations/resiliency/health-checks/app-health/#configuring-app-health-checks) and [K8s health checks](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/configuration/liveness-readiness-startup-probes/#configure-probes) are opt-in, should there also be a flag to determine if these endpoints should be exposed (similar to expose_entry)?
As done with the `_is_ready()` func, can we also include a check for the health endpoint so we know that the agent is actually running healthy?
Can you convert all the `%s` into f-strings please?

Yes that would be great. If you can add both an ENV variable + a setting in the [agent execution config](https://github.com/dapr/dapr-agents/blob/main/dapr_agents/agents/configs.py#L403-L413). 
You can see the [agent observability config](https://github.com/dapr/dapr-agents/blob/main/dapr_agents/agents/configs.py#L497). We should let the priority be agent execution config > env variable > default

This will cause all agents where the runner isn't using `.serve()` to return false. 
Should add a check for the attr [fastapi_app](https://github.com/JeffreyJPZ/dapr-agents/blob/0a95ef87f6cc83aa68ce8fac49c8805048e4e04f/dapr_agents/workflow/runners/agent.py#L540)
This is fine. Both return paths return tuples as far as I can tell
You can rely on the non-public method for now. If it becomes an issue we can look at what needs to be done

Just out of curiosity, is f-string logging a ["bad practice"](https://docs.astral.sh/ruff/rules/logging-f-string/)? Here it probably doesn't matter since these lines are evaluated once, but wouldn't hot paths like [`_schedule_workflow`](https://github.com/dapr/dapr-agents/blob/bab804abcc622cb420ff973e79a8a51de3b24df6/dapr_agents/workflow/utils/subscription.py#L372) result in lots of unnecessary evaluations? This is probably a premature optimization though.
Would this be the same as `_is_ready` excluding checking pub/sub consumers and HTTP routes (and shutdown signals)?

If i'm not misunderstanding something, most CLI flags/K8s annotations aren't automatically made available as env variables in the app? Should we expect the env variable names to follow the [CLI flags/K8s annotations](https://docs.dapr.io/reference/arguments-annotations-overview/) (e.g. ENABLE_APP_HEALTH_CHECK, APP_HEALTH_CHECK_PATH)?

I couldn't find any places where this return value of this `register_http_routes()` was actually used 👀 

Is this being done only to keep consistency with `register_message_routes()`, or  will these status functions be used in the future?

I'd go with `ENABLE_APP_HEALTH_CHECK` and `ENABLE_APP_READY_CHECK`.
You are correct that CLI flags and annotations aren't available as env vars.
In the real deployment model you'd either serve them as env vars on the manifest or you'd mount a configmap as env vars with the details. 

We shouldn't let the path be overwritten imo.

When running locally you can just run `export ENABLE_APP_HEALTH_CHECK` in the terminal for it to be available as env var.
Hm. I think I was more referring to checking the internals of the agent. The agent could in theory be stuck in a workflow or throwing unrecoverable errors while this would still return `ok`
Can you please update these (and any other `%` formats in the affected functions) to f-strings too?
The tiny overhead is not worth the optimization versus the readability gained. f-strings are so much easier to read and review and maintain. It gives consistency across the string formattings
`logger.exception()` already includes `exc_info` by default, no need for this :)

This might be out of scope for this PR, but `_is_ready()` knows exactly why the readiness check failed and I think it would be useful to have that reason included in the details of this 503.

CC @CasperGN
Yeah, I did it for consistency reasons. My reasoning is that pub/sub consumers are stateful, whereas these HTTP handlers can be considered stateless.

~~At this point should I refactor the entire module(s) or just the affected functions?~~

Nvm, I'm guessing refactoring the entire module is probably out of scope.

Thats fair. My initial thoughts are on some sort of heartbeat mechanism/watcher thread. I'm also looking at [the unrecoverable error path in the subscription code](https://github.com/dapr/dapr-agents/blob/bab804abcc622cb420ff973e79a8a51de3b24df6/dapr_agents/workflow/utils/subscription.py#L611-L615), it looks like the consumer thread just logs the error and exits? Should there be self-healing by recreating consumer threads, or should it just report unhealthy and have K8s restart the container?

Some questions building off of my initial thoughts:
- What if the watcher thread goes down? Can it be reactivated somehow (maybe reminders?)
- How can one distinguish between a really long workflow and a "stuck" workflow, if that even is possible? AFAIK there's no "global" timeout for workflows (which I guess would defeat the point of a workflow), but maybe this can be configured on the agent somehow?

Regarding the subscription, I think it's fine to report unhealthy and restart. We gotta be careful not to interfere with the expected behavior of retries etc as that comes from the retry policies.

@JoshVanL do you have some input here about how we could probe the wf runtime to infer if it's "running healthy" or not?

> Nvm, I'm guessing refactoring the entire module is probably out of scope. 

Yeah, we'll get to that bit by bit as we go by. Don't spend time and effort on this PR on it, just as long as we make sure new strings are formatted as f-strings
Concur, at least surfacing it out to the caller would be beneficial
Will add some more tests to ensure the config resolution works
Will there be support for runtime config hot-reloading of tool execution modes? Definitely not in the scope of this PR though

Added a `ToolChoice` enum for consistency with the other config settings, but looking at some of the LLM interfaces, this might not work for all situations since `tool_choice` can be a [dict when forcing a specific tool](https://github.com/dapr/dapr-agents/blob/3f848e46f337cc9e5872ceb50645aec43f4b27e2/dapr_agents/types/llm.py#L262-L264)?
Should the default max number of iterations be a named constant? Its repeated in quite a few places
Yep, good catch.
I think this is a good idea for a later PR yes

Hm. This might need a bit more consideration. It looks like most providers actually use `tool_choice: Optional[Union[str, Dict[str, Any]]]` which leads me to think we should have that structure as well if we wanted to make this stronger typed.

Let's leave this for a follow up

I put constants for agent execution defaults in the agent config module. I also thought about making a separate constants file like the one for observability but I ran into issues with circular imports (we need the enum at runtime so most workarounds don't apply), so I went back to the first approach.
Added a TODO for this

Looks like there's only the [`wait_for_worker_ready` method](https://github.com/dapr/python-sdk/blob/a56c7b178482d24ddd1214e5b942a75556d39c18/ext/dapr-ext-workflow/dapr/ext/workflow/workflow_runtime.py#L226), but that's probably not enough

hiiii 👋 can you pls update to revise the order here to be
1. statestore runtime config so we can hotreload here
2. explicit instantiation args making sure there is not a None default check
3. env vars
there should also be "required" and "none" if you can pls add those here
are there helpers you can use/create here so you can share this logic with the base.py changes above for these checks?
looks like this needs clean up from a merge conflict pls
here too pls
can you mv these funcs to a agent_health.py or something along those lines pls? make sure to include the license header too
this should be configurable i believe pls
can this be configurable pls with this default

```suggestion
            logger.warn(
```
pls can we avoid nested funcs?

Should the paths really be overridable? We were discussing earlier about this:

> I'd go with `ENABLE_APP_HEALTH_CHECK` and `ENABLE_APP_READY_CHECK`. You are correct that CLI flags and annotations aren't available as env vars. In the real deployment model you'd either serve them as env vars on the manifest or you'd mount a configmap as env vars with the details.
> 
> We shouldn't let the path be overwritten imo.
> 
> When running locally you can just run `export ENABLE_APP_HEALTH_CHECK` in the terminal for it to be available as env var.

The above would make sense if `/livez` and `/readyz` were required, but from what I'm seeing they're just conventions?
Claude, Mistral, and others use "any" instead of "required" (I think)?

Needed to bump the version since `StrEnum` is only supported in >=3.11, and some tests rely on `str(enum)` being equal to the string value which only works in >=3.11. I think it was stale anyway since the pyproject file specifies >=3.11.

A lot of the logic relates to coercing/validating/applying config values, I think `ConfigFieldDescriptor` can be reused here and on the observability side too (currently it only seems to be used for runtime config).
Was this behaviour intended from the beginning? Had to split the config update in two which leads to a bit of redundant processing because of this, not sure if there's another way.

+from dataclasses import fields, is_dataclass
+from typing import Any, Callable
+
+from pydantic import BaseModel
+
+
+def is_pydantic_model(obj: Any) -> bool:
+    """Checks if the given object is a subclass of Pydantic's BaseModel."""
+    return isinstance(obj, type) and issubclass(obj, BaseModel)
+
+
+def is_supported_config_model(obj: Any) -> bool:
+    """Checks if an object is a supported configuration model (Pydantic, dataclass, or dict)."""
+    return obj is dict or is_dataclass(obj) or is_pydantic_model(obj)
+
+
+def get_model_fields(model: Any) -> Any:
+    """Returns field names for a model."""
+    if type(model) is dict:
+        return model.keys()
+
+    if is_dataclass(model):
+        return [f.name for f in fields(model)]
+
+    if hasattr(model, "model_validate"):
+        # Pydantic v2
+        return model.model_fields.keys()
+
+    if hasattr(model, "parse_obj"):
+        # Pydantic v1
+        return model.__fields__.keys()
+
+    raise TypeError(f"Unsupported model type: {model!r}")
+
+
+def get_model_factory(model: Any) -> Callable[..., Any]:
+    """Returns a factory function that takes a dictionary of values and creates a model instance."""
+    if type(model) is dict:
+        return lambda vals: dict(**vals)
+
+    if is_dataclass(model):
+        return lambda vals: type(model)(**vals)
+
+    if hasattr(model, "model_validate"):
+        # Pydantic v2
+        return lambda vals: type(model).model_validate(vals)
+
+    if hasattr(model, "parse_obj"):
+        # Pydantic v1
+        return lambda vals: type(model).parse_obj(vals)
+
+    raise TypeError(f"Unsupported model type: {model!r}")

+from __future__ import annotations

+import pytest

+from dapr_agents.workflow.runners.agent import AgentRunner

+# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+# AgentRunner health endpoint checks
+# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
+
+
+def _make_agent_runner() -> AgentRunner:
+    return AgentRunner(name="test-agent-runner", wf_client=MagicMock())
+
+
+def _make_mock_agent(is_started: bool, pubsub: Any = None) -> MagicMock:
+    agent = MagicMock()
+    agent.name = "mock-agent"
+    agent.is_started = is_started
+
+    if pubsub is None:
+        agent.pubsub = None
+    else:
+        agent.pubsub = MagicMock()
+
+    return agent
+
+
+@dataclass
+class _MockResponse:
+    status_code: int
+    detail: Any
+
+    def json(self) -> Any:
+        return self.detail
+
+
+class _MockFastAPI:
+    def __init__(self) -> None:
+        self.routes: list[dict[str, Any]] = []
+
+    def add_api_route(
+        self,
+        path: str,
+        endpoint: Any,
+        methods: list[str],
+        summary: str | None = None,
+        tags: list[str] | None = None,
+    ) -> None:
+        self.routes.append(
+            {
+                "path": path,
+                "endpoint": endpoint,
+                "methods": set(methods),
+                "summary": summary,
+                "tags": tags or [],
+            }
+        )
+
+
+@pytest.mark.asyncio
+class _MockClient:
+    def __init__(self, app: _MockFastAPI) -> None:
+        self.app = app
+
+    async def get(self, path: str) -> _MockResponse:
+        route = next(
+            (r for r in self.app.routes if r["path"] == path and "GET" in r["methods"]),
+            None,
+        )
+        if route is None:
+            return _MockResponse(status_code=404, detail={"detail": "Not Found"})
+
+        endpoint = route["endpoint"]
+
+        try:
+            result = await endpoint()
+            return _MockResponse(status_code=200, detail=result)
+        except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001
+            status_code = int(getattr(exc, "status_code", 500))
+            detail = getattr(exc, "detail", "Internal Server Error")
+            return _MockResponse(status_code=status_code, detail={"detail": detail})
+
+
+def _make_mock_fastapi_app() -> _MockFastAPI:
+    return _MockFastAPI()
+
+
+@pytest.mark.asyncio
+async def test_livez_ok():
+    """When the agent is started, livez reports 'ok'."""
+    runner = _make_agent_runner()
+    mock_fastapi_app = _make_mock_fastapi_app()
+    agent = _make_mock_agent(is_started=True)
+
+    with patch(
+        "dapr_agents.workflow.runners.agent.register_http_routes",
+        return_value=([MagicMock()], [MagicMock()]),
+    ):
+        runner.serve(agent, app=mock_fastapi_app, expose_entry=False)
+
+    client = _MockClient(mock_fastapi_app)
+    livez = await client.get("/livez")
+
+    assert livez.status_code == 200
+
+

+def test_register_message_handlers_returns_status_functions():
+    """Test that status functions are returned for each subscription."""
+    mock_client = create_mock_dapr_client(["messagepubsub"])
+
+    @message_router(pubsub="messagepubsub", topic="orders.created")
+    def handle_created(message: OrderCreated):
+        pass
+
+    @message_router(pubsub="messagepubsub", topic="orders.cancelled")
+    def handle_cancelled(message: OrderCancelled):
+        pass
+
+    loop = asyncio.new_event_loop()
+    try:
+        with patch(_PATCH_TARGET, return_value=mock_client):
+            _, status_functions = register_message_routes(
+                dapr_client=mock_client,
+                targets=[handle_created, handle_cancelled],
+                loop=loop,
+            )
+    finally:
+        loop.close()
+
+    # Should return two status functions
+    assert len(status_functions) == 2
+    assert all(callable(status_function) for status_function in status_functions)
